data.title: Nvidia's Jensen Huang: Open Weights and US AI Leadership
data.description:
Source: NVIDIA Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

In the 1980s, early open-source software pioneers challenged a mainstream view—that software could only progress when companies had strict control over the code. This movement promoted the establishment of a transparent ecosystem that allowed developers worldwide to study, modify, and improve software. Software developed by the open-source community now underpins much of the internet, forms the basis of systems used by the world's largest technology companies, and serves as the underlying support for the US military and federal agencies in conducting scientific research, cybersecurity, and other critical missions. Open source not only reduced software costs but also created a shared knowledge base upon which generations of American engineers and entrepreneurs built their institutional sovereignty.

The United States now faces a similar choice in the field of artificial intelligence.

Our AI leadership will not be judged by a single cutting-edge AI model, but by whether the United States has built a strong, open ecosystem and permeated every industry. This is crucial for creating opportunities for innovation and prosperity nationwide. It requires expanding AI accessibility, encouraging competition, building a robust application layer, and giving Americans greater control over the technologies they rely on. Open-weighted models—AI models that anyone can download, review, modify, and run on their own infrastructure—are a vital component of this foundation because they make advanced AI more accessible, adaptable, and widely available. Startups, established companies, universities, and public institutions can build applications on top of advanced models without having to train a model from scratch or pay the exorbitant price of cutting-edge models for every task. Open weights allow every organization to match the right model to the right task at the right cost—reserve cutting-edge computing power for truly frontier problems, while running efficient, specialized models everywhere else. It is this discipline that will ensure AI remains economically sustainable as its use scales to billions of everyday tasks. The way the US won the AI ​​era was by integrating AI into workflows in factories, hospitals, farms, classrooms, and street shops. Open weights also strengthen market competition—and competition is key to ensuring that the benefits of AI are widely shared, rather than concentrated in the hands of a few. By allowing numerous organizations to build, adapt, and deploy advanced models, open weights create competition not only among model developers but also among cloud providers, chips, applications, and services. This competition fosters innovation, lowers costs, and distributes the benefits of AI broadly throughout the economy. Open Weights also empowers customers with greater control. As organizations invest more in AI, they want assurance that they are not locked into a single vendor or lose the knowledge and capabilities they have accumulated over time. The Open Weights model provides this assurance by allowing organizations to control their own data, evaluate and adapt models to their specific needs, and deploy models wherever their business requires them. When organizations create value with AI, Open Weights enables them to own that value through self-improving models, specialized capabilities, and accumulated knowledge, thereby driving American sovereignty and prosperity. Admittedly, Open Weights does carry real and unique risks. Once a weight is released, it is beyond the control of the original developer, and modified versions are difficult to trace or reverse. But the right way to address this risk is not to ban Open Weights. In a world where cyber attackers use advanced AI, defenders need models with sufficient capabilities to detect, simulate, and respond to emerging threats. Open models broaden defenses, increase transparency, and allow vulnerabilities to be discovered and fixed by numerous teams. In fact, openness may be one of the most important paths to AI security and safety. Simply relying on closed models is not inherently secure: they can be compromised, misused, or fail in ways imperceptible to outsiders. Concentrating advanced AI capabilities behind a few closed models exacerbates this risk—it creates a few single points of failure, weakens competition, and places critical technologies in the hands of a few providers. Open weighted models, on the other hand, allow a broad community of researchers and developers to review model behavior, identify vulnerabilities, develop safeguards, and improve the model over time. As open-source software has demonstrated—transparency can be more secure than closed systems—AI security may depend on empowering more people to test and strengthen the models society relies on. It enables rigorous benchmarking and evaluation, red-team testing, and safeguards against real and verifiable threats, rather than the default assumption that closed systems are more secure. A robust AI ecosystem is not a given. Policymakers have a crucial window of opportunity. This includes expanding access to computing power for startups and researchers, investing in shared training assets (datasets, tools, evaluation frameworks), and maintaining diversity at the forefront by avoiding premature restrictions on open models—restrictions that stifle competition or drive innovation overseas. These measures must also focus on how a robust application layer can scale the sovereign use of AI across the economy. In shaping this ecosystem, policymakers should be careful not to confuse legitimate model development techniques with inappropriate appropriation. Distillation, the process of using the output of one model to help train or improve another, is a widely used technique for model improvement, evaluation, and validation. It continues the long tradition of learning from, building upon, and improving upon existing technologies—a tradition that has driven innovation since the rise of the open-source software movement. In contrast, the illegal extraction of value from closed models does raise legitimate concerns. These concerns should be addressed through targeted legal and commercial frameworks, rather than imposing blanket restrictions on technologies that play a crucial role in AI innovation. The AI ​​era can be a prosperous one. Making the right choices, open-weighted AI can expand opportunities, strengthen competition, extend America's technological leadership, mitigate risks, and ensure that the benefits of this extraordinary technology are widely shared throughout the economy. This future is worth building, and the United States should lead the way. Joint Signatory Organizations: Alliance of Innovators, Andreessen Horowitz Venture Capital, Arcee AI, Arena, Black Forest Labs, Box, Claude Strike, Dell Technologies, Emergence Capital, Hugging Face, IBM, Linux Foundation, Mariana Mining, Meta, Microsoft, Mistral, Mozilla, NVIDIA, Palantir, Perplexity AI, Reflection, Replit, ServiceNow, Telnyx, Y Combinator Startup Incubator.

data.title: Golden Web3.0 Daily Report | US Crypto Regulatory Clarity Bill Stalled
data.description:
DeFi data

1. Total market value of DeFi tokens: 63.952 billion US dollars

DeFi Total Market Cap Data Source: coingecko

2. Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours: $33.21

Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours. Data source: coingecko

3. Assets locked in DeFi: $761.82 billion$100 million

Top 10 DeFi Projects by Locked Assets and Total Value Locked. Data Source: defillama

NFT Data

1. Total Market Value of NFTs: $5.744 billion

2.24 hours NFT trading volume 877 million US dollars

Toutiao

US Crypto Regulatory CLARITY Bill Stalled, Senate May Delay Review Until After August

On July 24, according to Fortune magazine, the long-awaited US crypto industry digital asset regulatory CLARITY bill has stalled. Senate Majority Leader John Thune stated that he does not expect the Senate to pass the bill before the August recess. Recently, the connection between the Trump family and crypto businesses has become a focus of controversy. Democrats are demanding that the bill include ethical clauses restricting government officials from profiting through crypto transactions, arguing that the Trump family has earned more than $2 billion through memecoin and other crypto projects, posing a conflict of interest. Ron Hammond, Head of Policy and Advocacy at crypto company Windemute, stated that the biggest obstacle currently facing the bill is not a lack of support, but rather the political pressure of the US midterm elections.

He believes that there may still be a window of opportunity for Congress to push through legislation after the election. Analysts point out that with the November midterm elections approaching, the Democrats may regain control of the House of Representatives and expand their Senate seats, potentially changing the legislative environment for cryptocurrencies. The current formal legislative process may only be temporarily stalled.

      AI Hot Topics

      1. Newly Reigned Fields Medalist Jacob Announces Joining OpenAI

      On July 24th, Jacob Zimmerman, one of the newly awarded Fields Medalists, announced immediately after receiving the highest honor in mathematics in Philadelphia that he would join OpenAI, focusing on the field of artificial intelligence security. When asked about his future plans at the press conference after receiving the award, Zimmerman bluntly stated, "I think the world is changing. The mathematics profession as we know it, I don't think it will exist in its current form anymore." He also announced that he would join OpenAI.

      2. UBS: Doesn't Believe There's a "Bubble" in China's AI Industry On July 24th, Xiong Wei, a China Internet industry analyst at UBS Securities, stated that Chinese cloud vendors are investing more steadily, emphasizing ROI and sustainability, and do not believe there's a "bubble" in China's AI industry. Meanwhile, as model capabilities continue to improve and inference demands continue to increase, investment in China's AI industry will gradually increase. 3. Amazon (AMZN.O) Launches Next-Generation AI Assistant Alexa+ Amazon (AMZN.O) has launched its next-generation AI assistant, Alexa+, which features conversational capabilities and task management functions.

      4. Intel CFO: AI PC Business Revenue Grows 26% Quarter-on-Quarter

      On July 24, Intel's Chief Financial Officer stated that revenue from its AI PC business grew 26% quarter-on-quarter and now accounts for two-thirds of customer revenue.

      5. Stripe Reportedly in Talks to Acquire AI Model Aggregation Platform OpenRouter

      On July 24, sources stated that Stripe is in talks to acquire the AI ​​model aggregation platform OpenRouter, and the deal may be finalized soon. OpenRouter was previously valued at approximately $1.3 billion, but if sold, the transaction value could reach approximately $10 billion.

      ...

      DeFi Hot Topics

      1. Morgan Stanley Ethereum Trust Receives SEC Registration Approval Notice

      On July 24, according to Cointelegraph, Morgan Stanley Ethereum Trust received a notification from the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regarding the approval of its registration, marking another step forward in the registration process.

        2. BitMEX Faces Class Action Lawsuit on the Day It Announced Shutdown, Alleged to Lose 622.66 BTC

        On July 24, according to Cointelegraph, BitMEX faced a class action lawsuit on the same day it announced its closure. The plaintiffs accused the derivatives platform of fraudulently manipulating customer liquidations to seize users' Bitcoin collateral.

        Plaintiffs BKX Services Inc. and David Namdar filed a lawsuit in the Southern District of New York, alleging a total loss of 622.66 BTC due to forced liquidation on BitMEX. BKX reportedly lost at least 305.81 BTC, and Namdar reported over 316.85 BTC. The plaintiffs allege that BitMEX's internal trading team had access to customer privacy information and continued trading while servers were frozen, preventing ordinary users from accessing or closing positions. They further allege the platform profited from the forced liquidation through an insurance fund. The plaintiffs are seeking the return of the seized Bitcoin and compensatory and punitive damages, representing U.S. customers who purchased BTC swaps since July 23, 2018. BitMEX announced on the same day that it would cease operations on September 23, had stopped new registrations, and banned new positions since August 26.

        3. Phantom Announces Robinhood Chain Integration

        On July 24, the crypto wallet Phantom announced that it has integrated Robinhood Chain. Users only need to update the application to the latest version to use it.

        4. Ripple Launches Ripple Mint Platform, Allowing Institutions to Mint and Manage RLUSD via UI or API

        On July 24, according to Cointelegraph, Ripple launched the Ripple Mint platform, allowing institutions to mint, redeem, and manage the RLUSD stablecoin via a UI (user interface) or API.

        5.Uniswap v4 Launches Permissioned Pools

        On July 24th, Uniswap launched Permissioned Pools, a new hook standard in Uniswap v4 that supports permissioned asset trading via Automated Market Makers (AMMs), with compliance enforced directly on-chain. Permissioned pools are built in collaboration with on-chain asset management teams. Initial partners include Superstate, Securitize, and Dowgo. Unlike relying on front-end or off-chain compliance checks, these pools verify wallet approval before any exchange or liquidity operation. Issuers retain control over the permitted list, and approved users can directly trade assets previously unavailable on AMMs on-chain.

        In terms of collaboration, Superstate participated in developing permissioned pool standards for tokenized stocks and funds, Securitize ensured that tokens issued through the DS protocol could be compliantly traded on-chain, and Dowgo contributed ERC-3643 integration. Disclaimer: Jinse Finance, as a blockchain information platform, publishes articles for informational purposes only and does not constitute actual investment advice. Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: Ecosystems aren't designed: Base paid its dues over three years.
data.description: The recent surge in popularity of the Robin Hood Chain has stirred up considerable waves within the Ethereum Layer 2 scaling ecosystem. BASE, which has consistently ranked first in TVL metrics, is now feeling uneasy. Coinbase co-founder Jesse Pollak recently published a lengthy article online (see reference link at the end), reflecting on his experiences and lessons learned in building the BASE ecosystem over the past few years. Two points particularly struck me after reading this article: First, the social applications (including content tokens and the creator economy) that Jesse heavily supported have largely failed to achieve the results he anticipated. Second, applications that Jesse neglected—prediction markets, perpetual contracts, and asset tokenization—have seen a surge in popularity. Regarding the social applications that Jesse strongly supports, there was a first surge in popularity during the 2017 ICO boom, followed by a brief surge in 2021. During those booms, I observed related applications, but I remained uncertain about their potential to create new ecosystems and models within the Web 3 ecosystem, so my interest gradually waned. As for the later surge in popularity of prediction markets, perpetual contracts, and asset tokenization, as mentioned by Jesse, it seems somewhat surprising to me. This is because these applications existed for a considerable period before the current surge in popularity. Polymarket launched as early as 2020; the perpetual contract market (dYdX) launched even earlier, in 2019; and asset tokenization was already present when BM created Steemit. These applications aren't new; they've existed for a long time, but never really took off. This situation is strikingly similar to the early stages of the dot-com bubble in the late 1990s. At that time, a large number of internet applications (including e-commerce and pet services) emerged. But soon after the bubble burst, most of those projects went to zero. In this respect, they fared worse than the Web 3 projects mentioned above. But then, after 2000, especially after the rise of Amazon and Alibaba, those internet projects that had been tried and failed were revived. This time, they proved viable, had business models, and could make money. This is the price of being born at the wrong time. Perhaps such a story will repeat itself in the crypto ecosystem. Jesse's reflection further reinforces my understanding of this point: The formation and successful innovation of a business ecosystem are almost impossible to design or plan artificially. Even an ecosystem (social applications) heavily promoted by a company like Coinbase ultimately failed miserably due to a lack of market acceptance; the situation is even more dire for others. Therefore, for the development of a platform, the best approach is to treat every project in the ecosystem fairly, as if each project were a guest, and to create a uniform and positive ecosystem for all guests. This is the most suitable method for platform development. The lack of fairness in this regard is precisely what Jesse and the Base ecosystem have done most criticized in the past two years—applications that bring huge traffic and users to the BASE ecosystem receive almost no attention. It was only when these projects went to Robin Hood and received a warm welcome there that Base felt immense pressure. However, despite the Base team's varying attitudes towards ecosystem projects over the past two years, there's one thing I haven't quite figured out: Base is arguably the most dynamic among many Layer 2 extensions. But it seems indifferent to many popular projects; where does its vitality come from? Especially since many hackers and makers enjoy innovating on BASE, and some well-known hackers have even compared BASE to a new Ethereum, believing it possesses strong geek characteristics and appeal. If BASE were the only ecosystem in Layer 2 scaling, many issues would be difficult to understand. Now, with a strong competitor, it's entirely beneficial to the development of the Ethereum ecosystem.

data.title: Is Kaito about to be repriced after X's first collaboration on an AI Data project?
data.description: After X officially announced its partnership with Kaito, the market's first reaction was natural: Is Kaito shifting from a simple InfoFi narrative to a broader AI Data narrative? This question deserves serious consideration. This is because it's not simply a "routine collaboration announcement" or a case of "Yaps's mechanism preparing for another round of widespread online activity." From the perspective of a Web3 market observer, this is more like a key data choice within Musk's AI strategy, signifying Kaito's rise to the top of the AI ​​Data infrastructure race. I. Musk's AI War is Changing X's Data Selection Criteria To understand this collaboration, we must first look at the massive AI battle being waged by the Musk system. Recently, multiple media outlets reported that SpaceX agreed to acquire Anysphere, the parent company of Cursor, for $60 billion. Whether from the perspective of AI programming entry points, developer workflows, or high-quality code data, this illustrates one thing: the Musk system is willing to pay extremely high costs for truly promising AI tools and data capabilities. Grok faces competition not only from general-purpose large models like ChatGPT and Claude, but also from AI tools like Claude Code and Cursor that are changing developer workflows. The AI ​​competition has moved beyond simply comparing model capabilities to a new phase of vying for high-quality tool entry points, user behavior, and vertical data. In this context, the collaboration between X and Kaito is more than just "adding another Web3 project." Kaito represents another, more fundamental and scarcer asset: real-time data, attention streams, creator signals, and narrative shifts. It answers the question: why is the market starting to discuss something, and who is driving that discussion? This is precisely one of the scarcest data assets in the AI ​​era. II. Why was Kaito chosen at this time? In the current AI war, model parameters and computing power are no longer the only barriers. What is truly scarce is high-quality data, especially real-time data, human behavior data, creator data, market sentiment data, and narrative shift data. The crypto market is precisely one of the most challenging yet most valuable data scenarios to process. Information here is too fast, too fragmented, and too contextual. Discussions on X, governance forums, research articles, Telegram, Farcaster, podcasts, conference transcripts—none of these are complete on their own, but combined, they can influence project valuations, trading sentiment, and market narratives. Ordinary search engines struggle to handle this information flow, and ordinary AI models also find it difficult to naturally understand the implicit signals within the crypto community. This is where Kaito's value lies. It's not a small, temporary plugin for X, but rather a vast database of crypto information accumulated over the past few years based on tracking and creator data from Kaito Pro, Yaps, and MindShare. This database stems from Kaito's product positioning: long-term indexing and analysis of Web3 information sources such as X, governance forums, research, news, podcasts, conference transcripts, Telegram, Farcaster, and Medium, continuously tracking the relationships between projects, tokens, narratives, creators, and market sentiment. Therefore, Kaito doesn't just "have a data entry point," but already possesses historical corpora, real-time signals, and semantic structures of the crypto world. For X and Grok, this capability is very direct: X has one of the largest real-time content repositories on the entire web, but the vast majority of this content is crude, unstructured data. What the platform truly needs is not more content, but a system capable of noise reduction, identification, attribution, and structuring. Which are genuine, high-weighted signals, and which are noise created by bots and spam? Which new trends are gaining momentum? Who is truly influencing the market, rather than just generating interaction? These are precisely the issues Kaito has been addressing for a long time. III. From InfoFi to AI Data: Kaito's Strategic Upgrade Many people's memories of Kaito are still stuck in the Yaps era. Posting, ranking, points, and airdrop expectations—when InfoFi was at its peak, Kaito was basically the representative of the field. But once that model got out of control, it easily turned the social platform into a "post farm": low-quality content, AI-generated spam, and replies proliferated, even crossing X's policy red lines. But the most crucial contrast in this collaboration is that X doesn't reject InfoFi, but rather the uncontrolled noise. What the platform truly needs is a methodology to refine the mixed traffic into high-quality Signals. This is precisely the core leap that Kaito has made in completing its strategic upgrade. Kaito Pro is essentially an AI-driven encrypted information and data analysis platform. Its features, such as MetaSearch, Narrative Mindshare, Token Mindshare, Sentiment Analytics, Smart Alerts, Dashboards, and Catalyst Calendar, all focus on one thing: refining the most chaotic, fragmented, and real-time encrypted information into high-purity structured data that is searchable, monitorable, interpretable, and readable by machines. This isn't just about providing traders with a simple trending list. It's more like an AI Data Production System: continuously analyzing what the market is discussing, who is driving the discussion, how sentiment is changing, which narratives are emerging, and how these signals affect projects, tokens, and communities. For Kaito, it's no longer just a marketing tool for distributing points outside of X, but rather, through its AI data capabilities, it's upgraded to a "real-time AI Data middleware layer" within X's official cooperation framework. It no longer teaches users how to "spam," but rather teaches the AI ​​model how to understand true human attention. IV. More Than Just X: Kaito Has the Potential to Become the Real-Time Mental Interface that AI Companies Are Eagerly Requesting. If we broaden our perspective further, we'll find that this collaboration is far more than just Kaito being useful to X or the crypto community. Any AI company aspiring to possess "real-time trend awareness" needs this data transformation capability. Consider the current shifts in AI business competition: Musk's system is willing to invest heavily in AI tools like Cursor, essentially vying for access to high-quality tools, user behavior, and vertical data. AI competition has fully entered a "battle for high-quality vertical data." Platform X, in essence, is one of the most frequent carriers of real-time human emotions, trends, slang, and market attention across the internet. Its biggest pain point is the rampant proliferation of unstructured data, rife with spam, bots, duplicate content, and low-quality noise. Large models directly capturing this information would be fed a massive amount of junk signals. How can real-time social networks be refined into machine-readable, computable, and weighted sentiment and attention data? This isn't just a problem for xAI or Grok. OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and any AI company looking to enter real-time search, financial information, the creator economy, prediction markets, and vertical industry intelligence will encounter the same bottleneck. X and Grok are simply the first scenarios to bring this essential need to the forefront. Just as Cursor made the market rediscover the strategic value of "code workflows and developer data," Kaito's core value shouldn't be limited to encrypted posting points. Its greater purpose is to establish a foundational capability that "transforms unstructured social network traffic into high-purity AI real-time mind maps." While future AI giants are vying to buy, bind, or monopolize high-quality data streams in vertical fields, Kaito is upgrading from "X's partner" to "the data infrastructure for the entire AI industry to capture encrypted real-time attention and trends." The potential of this kind of universal AI essential data source is orders of magnitude greater than simply earning points for posting within the crypto community. V. The Real Reasons for Kaito's Repricing Capital markets never trade just objective facts, but rather the repricing reasons following a narrative shift. This collaboration provides the market with three highly insightful revaluation logics. First, its niche is absolutely scarce. X is extremely selective in its official collaborations. Kaito became one of the few data projects to enter X's official collaboration framework, and it was also the first project X presented in the AI ​​Data/InfoFi direction. This means it has taken the lead in securing the golden entry point from "real-time social data to AI structured data." Second, its strategic evolution has been validated. Kaito experienced growing pains due to X's governance of rewarded posts and low-quality content, but it didn't remain stuck in the old InfoFi post-to-earn mechanism. Instead, through products like Kaito Pro, Studio, and Narrative Mindshare, it repositioned itself as a higher-quality data infrastructure. This isn't a simple "business recovery," but a leap from a marketing tool to AI Data infrastructure. Third, the new rules of the AI ​​era are clear. Incentives for spamming, low-quality traffic, and AI spam are unacceptable; verifiable, attributable data systems that directly help AI and platforms identify high-quality signals are acceptable. X has personally drawn this boundary for AI Data, and Kaito is currently the first project to cross it. In conclusion, this repricing of $KAITO is essentially a complete upgrade of the narrative. It's no longer just the old InfoFi story of "everyone posting to earn points," but a new story of "how AI infrastructure can extract, identify, and distribute real-time online attention and social data." The collaboration with X is just the beginning. When all AI companies are scrambling to acquire high-quality real-time data at all costs, and when model competition shifts from parameters and computing power to vertical data and real-time semantic understanding, Kaito has secured more than just a collaboration announcement; it has secured the first prime position in the trillion-dollar AI Data sector, a position whose pricing and valuation have been restructured by the market.

data.title: Opinion: The next crypto revival may begin with a "seed round IPO".
data.description:
Author: knimkar   Translator: Plain Language Blockchain

I don't believe perpetual contracts, stablecoins, or prediction markets will bring crypto back. The so-called "crypto resurgence" truly hinges on the emergence of on-chain assets that people believe they can profit from and that are simply unavailable elsewhere. It's that simple.

I believe the most likely path to achieving this again is an upcoming trend I call "Seed IPOs." That is, leveraging @MetaDAOProject's Ownership Coins and combining them with decision markets to create public trading markets for early-stage companies.

My judgment is based on the following beliefs: 1/ I expect more people to try starting businesses in the future because AI models are reducing the time and cost of creating things by orders of magnitude. This is self-evident. 2/ For many—even most—companies, gaining distribution capabilities is the hardest thing. In this close-quarters battle, Ownership Coins provide founders with a new distribution tool: Tokens. Anyone who has been in the crypto industry knows how powerful the "user-owner" mechanism is. Someone who already likes your product will become an extremely enthusiastic supporter if they can now share in the profits. A cynical crypto professional might say, "We've seen how ICOs ended up." But there's a huge difference between what Ownership Coins represent and what past tokens represented. 3/ I believe the continued expansion of the equity market will increasingly make public market participants uncomfortable. Traditional equity IPOs now look more and more like a harvesting event (look at SPCX structures). It's just BN ICO moved to the NYSE. The issue of "retail investors being unable to participate in OpenAI/Anthropic" has been discussed far too much—at least without those outrageous SPVs, retail investors are basically inaccessible—so I won't elaborate further. 4/ Retail investors now have access to a large number of highly volatile "gambling-like financial products": ODTEs, mobile casinos, sports betting, prediction market versions of sports betting, and perpetual contracts. They have long been trained to chase high-variance results. Instead of continuing to play those purely negative EV games, I can easily imagine the tide turning in the other direction: retail investors will invest in companies they like, use, and are willing to support, and they might even have an advantage here—because they are already heavy users. Current securities laws make it virtually impossible for early-stage companies to trade publicly. Ownership Coins and decision-making markets offer an alternative: they allow capital formation to continue while preserving some investor protection. MetaDAO is increasingly demonstrating this. In summary, I believe Ownership Coins meet the needs of the growing entrepreneurial community (corresponding to points 1/ and 2/ above) and also provide a unique opportunity for investors, especially retail investors (corresponding to points 3/ and 4/). Remember, crypto relies on two of the strongest drivers, in order: A/ Price increases (see Bitcoin) B/ Access to financial products people truly want but previously couldn't obtain (see Tether, Hyperliquid) In my view, MetaDAO and Ownership Coins have provided a solution for B/: enabling people to access early-stage companies' "seed-round IPOs." Now we're just waiting for one Ownership Coin to reach a market capitalization of $100 million to $1 billion, thus fulfilling condition A/. Once MetaDAO achieves a sufficiently large result, I believe the way early-stage companies raise funds will change. If this sounds unlikely, consider this: IPOs themselves have evolved, incorporating retail allocations, and I believe this trend will only continue. What I'm trying to say is that this trend will further spill over to seed-stage companies. You can think of it as a more mature version of the 2017 ICO frenzy. I can't wait. We're really coming back. Appendix: Yes, yes, I know you're going to say "adverse selection." I admit, the history of crowdfunding and launchpads isn't pretty, and adverse selection issues always seem to surface in various ways; I also admit that I'm saying "this time is different." But I genuinely believe this time is different. Below, I'll address some common objections to Ownership Coins.

Adverse selection [1]: Good founders will take the traditional VC route, only bad founders will do seed round IPOs.

Yes, this is the biggest risk. Top VCs not only pick winners, they also create winners. I think in the future you will see "good VCs" also participate in seed round IPOs, and MetaDAO's issuance has become increasingly obvious in this direction (VC + retail investors). So I think that truly high-quality seed round IPOs are likely to present a pattern of coexistence of brand VCs and retail investors. IPO book-building and underwriting is a mature process, we just put the company at an earlier stage.

Adverse selection [2]: Good founders will not be willing to let the decision-making market restrict their decision-making power.

This issue requires a longer discussion, but simply put, I think that over time we will gradually find a decision-making market balance that is friendly to investors and also friendly to founders. MetaDAO is constantly adjusting the design of its decision market based on feedback (for example, the team's proposals are now gaining a slight advantage). I'm not sure how the parameters of the decision market should ultimately be set optimally, but I think this direction can be gradually optimized.

Adverse selection [3]: The equity market is much larger and has a much higher ceiling than the token market. Good founders will not want to limit their upside potential.

Of course, but I think that as long as the asset is good enough, market participants will buy it, whether it is equity or token. Bitcoin has grown into a trillion-dollar asset; Hyperliquid has also become a huge asset, and so on. Again, "rising" itself is an incredibly powerful driving force.

Adverse selection [4]: ​​Good founders will not choose to make their assets publicly traded because that would be distracting. The benefits of the "user-owner" relationship may outweigh the distraction costs of owning a publicly traded asset. At least in my view, this isn't an obvious conclusion: whether or not there's a publicly traded asset will drastically change the final outcome. I've repeatedly seen how pressure can create diamonds. After speaking with the founders of MetaDAO, I felt that many people actually see it as a scoreboard, a source of motivation. Ownership Coins are simply a way to circumvent securities laws. Yes, that's true. But in my opinion, this isn't a bug, but a feature. The best products in crypto often have a strong regulatory arbitrage element. And that's part of the reason they succeed. Retail investors are too impatient for VCs. Retail investors are indeed impatient, but I don't think that's important. For retail investors, volatility is the product itself. And early-stage companies are inherently volatile throughout their lifecycle. My argument isn't that retail investors will become adept at VC investing; I'm simply suggesting they will participate, and likely on a large scale.

data.title: a16z Partner: It's time to pass the CLARITY Act.
data.description:
Source: Chris Dixon, Partner at a16z crypto; Translated by: Jinse Finance Claw

>

Every generation has the opportunity to upgrade infrastructure.

In the 1990s, the internet emerged. Policymakers worried it would disrupt existing industries and bring new risks. But instead of cramming the technology into outdated regulatory frameworks, they created rules that both fostered innovation and protected consumers. As a result, the United States experienced one of its strongest periods of economic growth in history.

Today, blockchain networks offer us similar opportunities. Stablecoins make payments faster and cheaper, and tokenization is driving the modernization of capital markets. Both rely on blockchain infrastructure.

These technologies will evolve regardless of Congress's involvement. Whether the United States sets the standards or cedes this field to others is up to the United States itself.

The Genius Act Demonstrates Clarity's Power. The GENIUS Act showcases the achievements of thoughtful policymaking. Stablecoins are a form of digital dollar that can circulate online as easily as Bitcoin. Sending $200 from the US to Colombia through traditional channels might cost over $12 and take several days. With stablecoins, the same remittance costs only a few cents and is settled in seconds. For years, regulatory uncertainty has hindered the adoption of stablecoins. The GENIUS Act changed this. By establishing reserve requirements and an issuing agency framework, the Act eliminated all doubts and unlocked the growth potential of stablecoins. The result speaks for itself. The stablecoin market size is approximately $315 billion, an increase of over 50% compared to a year ago. Dollar-backed tokens have become one of the fastest channels for the dollar to circulate overseas. According to Visa, stablecoin trading volume reached $100 trillion in the past 12 months. Major institutions including JPMorgan Chase, Citigroup, Visa, Mastercard, and BlackRock are deeply exploring blockchain infrastructure.

Crucially, the US dollar is winning. Millions of people worldwide who previously lacked access to traditional dollar accounts can now hold and trade digital dollars through software wallets. Clear rules have helped this new system take root in US standards and institutions, rather than competing alternatives.

Project GENIUS demonstrates that wise regulation can expand rather than restrict markets. But this only reveals part of the problem.

The GENIUS project proves that wise regulation can expand, rather than restrict, markets.

But this only reveals part of the problem.

Blockchain Networks Still Lack Rules

Stablecoins account for less than 15% of the cryptocurrency market capitalization, yet they rely on the remaining 85% of the underlying blockchain networks, which currently lack a unified regulatory framework. This is equivalent to regulating smartphones while ignoring cellular networks.

The CLARITY Act corrects this problem.

The core of CLARITY is to provide clear rules for blockchain networks and clarify regulatory responsibilities in the digital asset market. It incentivizes transparency, reduces risk, and promotes competition through a set of common standards. CLARITY empowers regulators to regulate intermediaries using principles proven effective in traditional finance, such as proper custody, segregation of client assets, and full disclosure, thereby helping to prevent a recurrence of disasters like FTX. It also ignites the next wave of institutional adoption of blockchain technology.

Major companies have already begun to act. BlackRock has launched a tokenized fund. JPMorgan Chase is building a blockchain-based payment system.

The DTCC, with $114 trillion in assets under custody, is preparing to expand the scale of tokenized securities through the Canton network. CLARITY eliminates barriers to entry, providing traditional financial institutions with a clear path to compliant participation. This benefits not only the cryptocurrency industry but also consumers, investors, and the long-term competitiveness of the US capital market.

The Choices We Face

History shows that open, neutral, and clearly defined platforms create the greatest value. The internet succeeded because entrepreneurs understood the rules of the industry. They were able to build platforms, attract capital, and compete based on merit, not regulatory speculation. Blockchain networks should have the same opportunity.

The opposition between regulation and innovation is a false proposition. The GENIUS Act has refuted this view: in the second half of 2025—after the GENIUS Act officially takes effect—more than $13 billion will flow into cryptocurrency startups, almost double the $6.9 billion invested before the GENIUS Act took effect in the first half of that year. Meanwhile, forecasts show that the tokenized asset market will grow 100-fold in the coming years.

The choice facing the United States is between certainty and uncertainty: it can choose to take a leading role and build the future domestically, or it can watch that future be built elsewhere. No law is perfect, and the CLARITY Act is no exception. The reality is that the cryptocurrency sector currently lacks consumer protection. The CLARITY Act aims to establish these protections. No bill can please everyone, but the version released today will move the industry forward. It reflects months of bipartisan negotiations and significant compromises made by the industry. Regardless of its shortcomings, the CLARITY Act is far better than continuing without any safeguards. The decisions made by Congress this year will determine the direction of the next generation of financial infrastructure and the rule-makers. If the CLARITY Act passes, the United States will once again lead financial development, just as it once led the commercial internet. If Congress does not act, innovation will move elsewhere and follow frameworks established by other countries.

data.title: Interview with Zeng Xinxun, founder of Liangpei: After Kimi left, he started working as an AI matchmaker
data.description:
During WAIC, Zeng Xinxun's product did not appear on any of the booths. His only public introduction of "Liangpei" at the event was during a flash debate organized by the WeChat Mini Program team. The topic was: Should AI-powered relationship products allow users to "use and leave," or should they pursue "long-term stickiness"? Zeng Xinxun chose the former. In an industry where all products try their best to retain users, he believes that the true success of dating apps is for users to find the right person quickly and then leave. AI should not be a substitute for a long-term relationship; it should step back after the match is complete. A year ago, Zeng Xinxun left Kimi to start this AI dating product called "Liangpei". Before that, he worked on search and recommendation at WeChat and TikTok, and his last job was as the head of AI search technology at Kimi. Search solves the problem of matching information with needs. Now, he wants to replace documents with people. To enter the matching system, users first need to have a 20-minute voice conversation with the AI, allowing the model to understand their personality, experiences, and mate selection criteria. The AI ​​then compares the profiles of two people and analyzes the probability of mutual compatibility. After entering the communication phase, it can also act as an "avatar" and "strategist," answering awkward questions, reading chat history, and helping people determine the progress of a relationship. However, the truly unique aspect of this product isn't entirely in the AI. Liangpei requires users to fill out their profiles carefully, allows matching with only one person at a time, doesn't restrict the exchange of WeChat contacts, and even offers a "money-back guarantee if you don't get married within three years" membership program. Almost every design element actively abandons the registration conversion, activity, and retention metrics valued by traditional internet products. Why would someone who used to work in search want to use AI to handle intimate relationships? What should AI do for humans, and where should it stop? How does a product that wants users to leave as quickly as possible ultimately plan to monetize? We talked to Zeng Xinxun. Products grow from problems. This isn't Zeng Xinxun's first entrepreneurial venture. More than ten years ago, he was a student at Southern University of Science and Technology. At that time, food delivery platforms had just emerged, and he and his classmates started an online restaurant called "Nanfeng Diandian," delivering cooked food to university dormitories. This story also stems from his own life. He's a heavy user of food delivery services. In an era when you still needed to flip through menus, make phone calls, and repeatedly explain your address, he felt ordering a meal shouldn't be so complicated. So he restructured the process of offline restaurants and tried to complete ordering, preparation, and delivery online. More than ten years later, he entered the dating and marriage market, which already had a large number of products and had existed for more than twenty years, starting from his own life again. First encounter a problem, then create a product. This is the entrepreneurial approach that Zeng Xinxun is most familiar with. Zeng Xinxun's first entrepreneurial venture targeted the university market, focusing on delivery to the bed, and orders would surge on rainy days. Beating: When you first started your campus food delivery business, there weren't any particularly dominant platforms in the market. Now, you're in the dating industry, facing a market that's been around for over twenty years and has a plethora of products. What are the similarities between these two ventures? Zeng Xinxun: All my product ideas actually come from my own life. I started a food delivery business because I'm a super heavy user of food delivery services. I probably order over a hundred times a year. Back then, ordering food delivery involved spending half a day communicating over the phone, and I felt this process was too complicated. There should be a more reasonable, more digital solution, so I started doing it. At the time, we were operating in several universities in Guangdong, accumulating a considerable user base. Initially, I didn't think about platforms, competition, or the future market landscape. My competitor wasn't another food delivery platform, but the old experience of ordering food by phone. Later, large platforms like Meituan entered the market, raising funds and subsidies, burning through hundreds of millions or even billions of yuan, which is how we were driven out. That's a story for later. The same goes for Liangpei; its inspiration came from my own blind date experiences. The difference is that dating is a sector with over twenty years of history, with platforms like Zhenai.com and Jiayuan.com, and later, a series of dating products such as Soul, Momo, and Tantan. I myself was a heavy user of these products, and I felt they didn't truly solve my problems. With the advent of AI, I believe this experience can be completely transformed. Beating: What was your experience finding a partner like? Zeng Xinxun: I took a three-year break from school when I started my first business, so I was already 25 when I graduated with my bachelor's degree. Looking back, I realized that from 18 to 25, I was either starting a business, working, or preparing for graduation, and I hadn't had a serious relationship. I felt it was a real pity. If 20 to 30 is a precious period in life, half of that time had already passed, and I was still single. So after graduation, I really made "finding a partner" my KPI. At first, I looked around, seeing if there were any suitable people among my colleagues and classmates. After searching around, I found that most of the people I admired already had partners. Then I started participating in various activities. I participated in company-organized hikes, mountain climbing, badminton, frisbee, werewolf games, and murder mystery games—any opportunity to meet new people. I also attended social events organized by the school alumni association and posted on the company's internal dating forum. Having tried offline methods without success, I registered on a dating app. I spent over a year on it but still didn't meet the right person. Finally, I considered giving up and uninstalled the app. A few months later, it was New Year's Day again. I realized another year had passed, and I still hadn't achieved my goal, so I felt a little unwilling and reinstalled the app. After logging back in, I saw that I had only received one message in the past few months—from my current wife. We chatted online for a day, met the next day, and got together that same day. Now, six years have passed, and we have a one-year-old daughter. This process made me realize that finding a truly suitable partner today is not an easy task. Whether you look within your immediate circle, expand your network through events, or use an online platform that seems to offer many options, there are many difficulties. These difficulties were difficult for technology to handle in the past because interpersonal relationships are so non-standardized. I think the fuzzy logic of large models has, for the first time, the opportunity to address some of these issues. A Luxurious Two Thousand Words When using dating apps, Zeng Xinxun wrote himself a personal introduction of over two thousand words. It included his personality, family, education and entrepreneurial experiences, as well as future plans, lifestyle habits, diet, and daily routine. He wanted to present himself as completely as possible. If there was anything the other person couldn't accept, they could eliminate him as early as possible. After writing it, he received fewer "likes." But it was these two thousand words that ultimately led him to the right person. One of his wife's classmates saw his profile first and forwarded it to the research group group. She registered for the software because of this profile and sent him a message. However, Zeng Xinxun had already uninstalled the app by then and only saw it several months later. Both of them wrote very long descriptions of themselves. Zeng Xinxun wrote over two thousand words, and his wife wrote over one thousand. They didn't meet because they had too many choices; precisely because they were both willing to seriously reveal themselves. Beating: What do you think was the core problem with past dating or matchmaking apps? Zeng Xinxun: The first problem was that users provided too little information. On many past dating apps, besides a few photos and some tags, it was difficult to truly understand a person. This lack of information stemmed from two issues: firstly, a lack of willingness—many people didn't have high expectations for dating apps and were unwilling to invest; and secondly, a lack of ability—not everyone could logically and systematically introduce themselves, and many people probably didn't know themselves very well. So, you could only gradually increase your understanding through chatting and meeting in person. This process required three things. The first was time. You might spend a weekend afternoon meeting someone. Secondly, there's the cost—food, taxis—all have expenses. Thirdly, and most importantly, there's the mental and emotional investment. You need to enthusiastically introduce yourself to a stranger, patiently listen to them, and show interest in their life. But ultimately, you're likely not a good match, and all that investment becomes a sunk cost. My patience, time, and emotions should be reserved for those who deserve more. There's no need for everyone to go through the entire process of trial and error on their own. AI can at least help provide more context and then filter out obviously unsuitable people before the meeting even begins.

Liangpei's AI Voice Interaction Page

Beating: But getting users to fill in more information doesn't just happen naturally with AI. How exactly does Liangpei improve the richness of the information?

Zeng Xinxun:We turned filling in the information into an AI-guided voice dialogue.

Not everyone can write a short essay, but if someone talks to you for 20 minutes, most people can share a fairly good picture of their situation, provided they're willing to cooperate. What kind of person are you? Do you prefer stability or adventure? Do you want to continue building your career or lead a relatively peaceful life? What are your views on marriage, children, and cities? These can all be gradually developed during the conversation. We've internally analyzed some competitors, such as Qing Teng Zhi Lian and Qian Shou, and their average user profiles are only about 100 words long. Liangpei's current user profiles average over 400 words. When a person is no longer just a few photos and tags, but becomes a more three-dimensional individual, both parties have a basis for judging whether they are compatible. Beating: When people introduce themselves, they often subconsciously embellish their self-image. How can AI distinguish between a person's self-image and their true self? Zeng Xinxun: We can't completely distinguish them, and I don't think it's our obligation. In normal interpersonal interactions, everyone embellishes themselves slightly. When you see someone's self-description, you'll naturally discount it a bit. Someone might say they're 1.7 meters tall, but they might be 1.68 meters; someone might say they're 1.8 meters tall, but they might be 1.75 meters. But you need at least an original price before deciding how much to discount. What we do is help people give a more complete and comprehensive "original price." As for the true situation, it still needs to be confirmed step by step through subsequent interactions. This is better than having no information at all and having to guess from scratch. Beating: With more data, why can't traditional products still solve the matching problem? Zeng Xinxun: Even with a lot of data, past products might not be able to understand it. I wrote two thousand words, but the platform didn't recommend me to people who might actually appreciate that content. Was my wife the only person in Shenzhen who might like me? Definitely not. The reason is that for traditional recommendation algorithms, two thousand words might just be a piece of text of two thousand characters. They can extract keywords, tag it, and then perform tag matching, but it's difficult to understand the underlying meaning. For example, I've written about my entrepreneurial experience, which reflects that I might enjoy taking risks, but it could also mean that my life isn't very stable. Some people might see this and think it's unsuitable, while others might like it. I once met a girl introduced to me by a teacher. We chatted on WeChat for a week, got along very well, and even became a little flirtatious. But after our first meeting, after I told her about my entrepreneurial experience, she went home and told me to forget it. She wanted a more stable life and didn't want to live in constant fear, worrying about losing her job or her business failing. But I've also met people who are completely the opposite. She doesn't like working in a company all the time and instead hopes her partner is willing to take risks. The same characteristic can be a disadvantage for some people and an advantage for others. If technology cannot understand this difference, it can only continuously orchestrate ineffective communication. When I worked on AI search at Kimi, the essence was to first understand the user's needs, then understand the document, and determine if the two could match. Now, it's simply replacing that document with another person. AI doesn't date people. AI's role in romantic relationships is delicate. If a product aims for long chat sessions, it's better suited to be a companion; if it aims for matching efficiency, it's more like a middleman; if it aims to provide more choices, it will constantly bring new people to the user; if it considers truly establishing a relationship as success, it must also know when to exit. The same technology, because of different service metrics, will ultimately serve completely different people. Zeng Xinxun defines the boundaries of AI as follows: it can understand, filter, relay, and remind, but it cannot replace a person in liking another person. AI handles the parts that don't require human intervention, leaving time, effort, and emotion for the parts that truly require human involvement.

Beating: In your opinion, which parts of a relationship should AI intervene in, and where should it stop?

Zeng Xinxun:AI is meant to assist people, not make decisions for them. It can't designate a person and tell them: You're a good match, so be together. That's ridiculous.

Beating: In your opinion, which parts of a relationship should AI intervene in, and where should it stop?

Zeng Xinxun:AI is meant to assist people, not make decisions for them. It can't just designate a person and tell them: You're a good match, so be together.

... A chemical reaction is needed between people. You need to meet, spend time together, and invest time to build a relationship. These can only be done by humans. However, not all the work in this process has to be done by humans. First, AI can help you explain the information clearly. Second, it can understand this information, perform searches, and make recommendations. Third, it can also act as a mediator during communication between two people. For example, we've set up an AI avatar in Liangpei (a matchmaking platform). Each user has their own avatar, and others can ask it questions. Some questions are repetitive, such as which city they plan to develop in the future, or whether they have marriage plans; some questions are more awkward, such as past romantic experiences, family situation, home purchase plans, whether parents have retirement funds, or views on bride price. If the answer is already in the user's profile, the AI ​​avatar can answer directly. If not, it will anonymously relay the question to the user to solicit an answer, and will adjust the wording, removing any expressions that might make the user uncomfortable. This both supplements a person's profile and avoids everyone repeatedly answering the same questions like customer service representatives. Beating: Besides answering questions for people, how does the AI ​​strategist intervene in conversations between two people? Zeng Xinxun: The AI ​​strategist will read both parties' profiles and chat history to provide suggestions to users. We have seen a pair of users... The boy and girl both asked the AI ​​the same question: "Based on our chat history, does the other person have feelings for me?" They both wanted to move the relationship forward, but neither was sure. In the past, you might have asked a friend or best friend, but that required explaining a lot of context, and even friends didn't know the other person well. The AI ​​advisor provided a relatively complete perspective in this scenario. We later planned to add a feature. If both parties asked similar questions, a hidden Easter egg would appear, called "Unanimous" or "Telepathic Connection," telling them: You're both actually very curious about each other now. What people really fear is being the only one invested, the fear of becoming the clown. Few people would mind a confirmed mutual attraction. Another girl was chatting very well with a guy, but on the third day, he suddenly stopped replying to her messages. She sent messages in the morning, noon, and evening, and then asked the AI: Why isn't he replying to me? The AI ​​initially told her that he was pursuing a PhD and might be busy, and that she could wait a little longer. The next day, she asked again how much longer she had to wait. The AI ​​told her she could wait a maximum of three days. If there was still no response after three days, it wasn't just that he was busy; it could also mean he wasn't interested in continuing the relationship. On the third day, she asked if she should unmatch the guy. The AI ​​suggested she end it, and she did. Later, when we followed up by phone, she said she really liked the guy's profile and was reluctant to give up easily. However, being in the thick of it makes it difficult to make decisions for oneself. AI gave her an outlet to review and exit. Beating: Aren't you worried that users will leave Liangpei after adding each other's contact information? Zeng Xinxun: Let them leave. Our goal is to solve users' needs for finding partners. If you exchange contact information, it means your relationship has progressed to the point where you need to switch platforms to chat, and we've already accomplished part of our mission. Why would I keep you around? Initially, some users were very cautious when exchanging contact information. They would send a WeChat QR code and then withdraw it, or send their phone number in three parts, perhaps because they were used to other platforms restricting referrals. Later, we added a prompt. When the system detects that a user has sent their WeChat ID or phone number, it will directly tell them that the platform does not restrict the exchange of contact information. It's a good thing that users have entered into a real relationship. Most internet products try to lower the registration threshold. Information can be filled in later, identity can be verified later; get users in first, then figure out how to improve activity and retention. Liangpei chose the opposite direction. Users need to complete approximately 20 minutes of AI dialogue and meet a certain standard of detail in their profile before they can officially enter the product. It also includes real-name authentication, unmarried status verification, and real-person profile picture verification. These steps will lower the registration conversion rate and cause some people who just want to browse to leave early. This is precisely the purpose of the design. Zeng Xinxun doesn't intend to persuade everyone to stay. He first determines what kind of people deserve to be served, and then keeps those who don't belong to this stage out.

Zeng Xinxun attends alumni event

Beating: What kind of users are you looking for?

Zeng Xinxun:Currently, we mainly target urban white-collar workers. This is because users need to express themselves clearly and be able to articulate their needs. This is related to age, education, and life experience.

But the most important requirement is only one: they genuinely want to find a partner. People's attitudes towards relationships differ at different stages of life. Sometimes it's just casual browsing, sometimes they're eager to get married, and sometimes they're preparing to seriously look for a partner. "Good Match" is only suitable for the last type of person. The first 20 minutes of conversation and profile review are designed to allow users who don't currently belong to this group to naturally leave. This way, those who remain on the platform at least know that both parties' expectations are aligned. Everyone is willing to seriously pursue a relationship with the goal of a long-term commitment, even marriage. Beating: Why does "Good Match" have a one-to-one matching system? Before confirming whether someone is suitable, users usually want to compare several options. Zeng Xinxun: Other platforms generally allow chatting with many people simultaneously. Users send "likes" to many people, and after the others reply, many chat partners appear in the list. They chat a few words here and a few words there, and in the end, they might not be able to chat with anyone. The mechanism of Liangpei is that you can send "likes" to different people, but as long as one person responds, the two enter a one-to-one matching state. At this time, neither of them will see other recommendations or receive new messages from others. They will only return to the recommendation list when one party unmatches them. AI can do the initial screening, but after finding a potentially suitable person, you need to invest time and focus to develop a relationship. In the past, no one believed they would be taken seriously. You didn't know if you were the tenth person on someone's list. We hope that AI and humans can form a division of labor. AI will do the initial screening and understanding work, while humans will do the later part that requires emotional investment. The premise of one-on-one is that you can't treat someone as a backup option. Beating: Won't this mechanism create pressure for people? Unmatching feels like directly rejecting someone. Zeng Xinxun: This is indeed a common user feedback issue. Many people rarely say no directly, so they feel psychological pressure when ending a relationship. But if you keep dozens of uncertain chat contacts because of pressure to refuse, the end result might be that no one gets taken seriously. My investor initially opposed the one-on-one model. She said that as an investor, she needs to review many projects and compare them repeatedly before investing in one. Why can't you look at other people after reviewing one when looking for a partner? My answer was that investors are in a relatively dominant position when reviewing projects; they are making the decisions. But in close relationships, both parties are equal. While you are choosing others, others are also choosing you. If everyone wants to keep more choices at the same time, they may end up in a common predicament. Everyone has many people on their list, but no one is willing to commit first. We talked about this issue for a long time and also conducted some user interviews. Finally, she accepted that one-on-one might be a very differentiated mechanism for finding a good match, and also a way to improve matching results. Beating: Will you extend this technology to finding friends, partners, or professional networking in the future? Zeng Xinxun: At least not in the Beating product. A product needs to solve one problem first before it can be continuously deepened around that problem. Some AI social products nowadays want to help users find friends, partners, lovers, and jobs. They try to do everything, but in the end, each relationship can only be very superficial. Because if any function is made too deep, it will feel redundant to users with other purposes. Liangpei prioritizes mastering the core of dating and matchmaking. This technology might be applied to other products later, but it won't cram all relationships into a single product. "Don't believe in user retention" isn't a new concept for dating products. Hinge, a dating app, has long defined itself as an app "designed to be deleted," and established Hinge Labs, staffed by behavioral researchers, to study what profiles, matches, and interactions are more likely to lead users to real dates. Liangpei takes this a step further. It not only doesn't equate retention with success, but also attempts to link revenue to the final outcome. Users who purchase the "Marriage Guarantee Membership Plan" can apply for a full refund if they don't register their marriage within three years. In Zeng Xinxun's design, this is a business model derived from product values. The platform cannot simultaneously hope for user success while relying on continued user engagement to generate revenue. However, just because values ​​can derive product mechanisms doesn't mean it's inherently a viable business. Beating: Why was "marriage" ultimately defined as product delivery, rather than matching, meeting, or establishing a romantic relationship? Zeng Xinxun: This model was derived step by step. We use AI, which consumes tokens, and we've invested a lot of resources to help users. The goal should be to make it easier for them to find a partner, not to make them stay on the platform longer. Given this, our business model cannot be solely linked to user time spent on the platform. Otherwise, we'll be contradicting ourselves: wanting you to succeed on one hand, but not wanting you to succeed on the other. Therefore, revenue should be related to results. But what constitutes success needs further definition. In the first version, we considered it success if users exchanged contact information and met offline. This step was very easy to verify; we just needed to see if both parties exchanged WeChat contacts. But users didn't accept this. They would say, "I only added them on WeChat, we haven't met in person yet"; or "We met and parted on bad terms, how can that be considered success?" Let's continue. Establishing a romantic relationship is generally considered a success. However, it's difficult to verify. Anyone can claim they haven't been in a relationship, making it impossible for the platform to confirm. Next comes marriage. Marriage is a mutually agreed-upon milestone and can be verified through marriage registration. Therefore, the final solution becomes marriage as the delivery. Regarding the timeframe, we conducted a small-sample survey of over a hundred registered marriage applicants. According to our survey results, if two people met after graduation and while working, rather than developing a relationship from classmates, most would decide whether to marry within three years of meeting. This led to the "money-back guarantee if you don't marry within three years." We hope the entire product and success rate form a closed loop. By improving matching and communication effectiveness, users are more likely to succeed, allowing the platform to generate revenue. Beating: Your team currently has 17 people, but none have a background in psychology or intimate relationship research. Relying solely on models and product experience, how do you ensure the matching logic is reliable enough? Xinxun Zeng: We don't have any yet, but we definitely will in the future. After Hinge reached a certain scale, we also established our own research team specifically to study intimate relationships and user behavior within the product. University psychology researchers have many theories, but the platform has firsthand data. How users meet, how they communicate, and how relationships develop—only the product team can continuously access this data. In the future, we also hope to establish a similar team to study how relationships are formed on the platform, and then use these results to further optimize the model. Our goal is for Liangpei to ultimately become one of the models that best understands intimate relationships. Products should have their own judgment. Zeng Xinxun roughly divides product managers into two categories. One type believes more in experience, intuition, and value judgment. The other type relies more on data, experimentation, and reusable methods. He also maps these two tendencies to his own work experience. At WeChat, he felt that products must first have stable values; at TikTok and ByteDance, he felt a lot of A/B testing and rapid iteration, leaving different solutions to data to judge. Liangpei is closer to the former. Many of its choices may not perform best in short-term data. A 20-minute voice call might lower registration rates, one-on-one interaction might reduce engagement, allowing WeChat exchanges might lower retention, and a refund policy for non-marriage might slow revenue growth. These choices depend on the founders making initial judgments and then using the product to validate those judgments.

2019-2022, Zeng Xinxun worked in Tencent's WeChat search team

Beating: You divide product managers into "experience-oriented" and "method-oriented" types, and you also apply this to your work experience at WeChat and ByteDance. What are the differences between these two approaches?

Beating: You divide product managers into "experience-oriented" and "method-oriented" types, and you also apply this to your work experience at WeChat and ByteDance. What are the differences between these two approaches?

Still from the TV show "Hang the DJ"

Image source: Douban

The position that Zeng Xinxun arranged for Liangpei is similar to this.

AI first helps users understand themselves, filters out obviously unsuitable people, and then puts two potentially suitable people in front of each other.

AI first helps users understand themselves, filters out obviously unsuitable people, and then puts two potentially suitable people in front of each other.

Once users start investing time, building rapport, and even switching to WeChat to continue chatting, the product should take a backseat. In this logic, user departure isn't a loss, but a success. This product logic is complete, but the business logic isn't fully established. If a user doesn't marry within three years, the membership fee needs to be refunded; during this period, the platform still bears the costs of tokens, authentication, services, operations, and customer acquisition. Marriage is influenced by family, city, economy, personality, and personal choices, making it difficult to attribute entirely to a single algorithmic match. A more realistic issue is that "Good Match" still needs to rapidly expand its user base. Dating matching heavily relies on user density within the same city, of similar age, and with similar needs. The 20-minute voice chat, one-on-one matching, and stricter verification thresholds will actively filter out some people. These designs can filter out those who don't take relationships seriously, but they may also make it harder for a product that already needs scale to achieve it. How to price memberships, how to manage funds, what revenue will cover costs over three years, and how the platform verifies whether a marriage is facilitated by a good match—these questions remain unanswered. Currently, "money-back guarantee if you don't get married" is more like a strong product promise than a proven business model. In "Hang the DJ," the match only truly works when two people leave the system. Liangpei also hopes users will leave in this way. However, it first needs to prove that it can stay until that day.

data.title: The suspense surrounding the implementation of Russia's new law on A7 and A5 has only just begun.
data.description: Russia has just enshrined the provision allowing foreign trade participants to use digital currencies for settlement in cross-border transactions into the main text of its law. However, in reality, a ruble-based stablecoin has been on this path for over a year—it's called A7A5. The new law aims to establish a compliant channel under the guise of the state; A7A5, on the other hand, is taking a completely different route. In February 2025, Old Vector, a company registered in Kyrgyzstan, minted its first batch of A7A5 stablecoins, pegged to the ruble, with reserve assets held in the core of the Russian defense system—Promsviaz Bank (PSB), which has been sanctioned by the West for many years. Beyond the issuing entity, the A7 network to which this stablecoin belongs has been identified by the US Treasury Department as being linked to Moldovan oligarch Ilan Shore. Shore himself was sentenced in absentia for a billion-dollar bank fraud case and faces sanctions from the US, UK, and other countries. According to reports, by January 2026, A7A5's cumulative on-chain transaction volume exceeded $100 billion, becoming one of the world's largest non-USD stablecoins in less than a year, with approximately 250,000 transactions and over 41,000 accounts. This origin destined it to be no ordinary stablecoin project from day one. The most counterintuitive aspect of A7A5's history is its reaction to sanctions—not shrinking, but expanding. In August 2025, the U.S. Treasury Department took action against Grinex, the main exchange for A7A5. However, contrary to expectations, the trading volume of this stablecoin did not shrink; instead, its market capitalization rose, briefly approaching $500 million, reaching more than 40% of the total market capitalization of non-USD stablecoins at the time. Reports citing on-chain data suggest that a significant proportion of tokens in the A7A5 network underwent destruction and reminting in new wallets after the associated exchanges were sanctioned. This method objectively severs the connection between frozen addresses and new tokens. However, it should be noted that the specific proportion and triggering timing currently lack cross-verification from official reports from first-hand on-chain analysis institutions such as Elliptic and TRM Labs. It remains a detail worth observing but has not yet been confirmed. Regardless of whether these specific operational details can ultimately be confirmed, a simpler fact suffices to illustrate the point: despite Garantex's shutdown and Grinex's sanctions, this coin didn't die; instead, it grew stronger. This at least demonstrates that relying on freezing a few exchanges to shut down a crypto settlement channel is far less effective than imagined. A7A5's survival to this day isn't solely due to these countermeasures; it also relies on a sophisticated legal framework. Russia has a Digital Financial Assets Law (259-FZ) from 2021 that allows licensed "information system operators" to issue regulated digital financial assets (DFAs), which must be linked to private blockchains and issued by licensed institutions like Sberbank and Alfa-Bank—sounds completely different from public blockchain stablecoins like A7A5. However, according to an analysis by the Harvard Davis Center, in October 2025, the Central Bank of Russia issued a "letter of no objection," which some research institutions interpreted as tacit approval by Russian regulators for the cross-border use of A7A5. It's important to note, however, that this is still the analytical wording used by research institutions regarding the Central Bank of Russia's actions, not an official characterization publicly used by the Central Bank of Russia itself. The official status of A7A5 is actually "Foreign-issued Digital Rights" (FDRs), which doesn't entirely align with the original design intent of 259-FZ, but regulatory agencies tacitly approved it. This is the path A7A5 has taken over the past two years: no specific law was tailored for it, but rather an ambiguous document from the central bank opened the door to cross-border settlements without prior consultation. The new Duma law essentially paved the way into a legitimate avenue—allowing the use of digital currency in foreign trade contract settlements is directly written into the law, eliminating the need for an ambiguous letter from the central bank. However, the new law establishes a domestic registration list: exchanges, clearinghouses, and brokers must all be registered with the Central Bank of Russia, and banks and financial institutions will face stricter compliance requirements. A7A5, on the other hand, is the opposite—its issuer is in Kyrgyzstan, but its trading venues are overseas exchanges and public blockchain DeFi protocols.
