Its cross-border structure objectively reduces the impact of a freeze in a single jurisdiction. This is the paradox the new law presents to A7A5: to reap the benefits of compliance, it needs to be registered domestically; but once a clear trace of its institutions and funds is left, its prized moat of "easily rebuildable and difficult to freeze" weakens. The new law seeks a visible and controllable national channel; the value of A7A5 lies precisely in its unseen and uncontrollable nature. Whether the two can be compatible is itself a question. After the ceasefire, who will be eliminated first? What truly worries A7A5 is not the sanctions themselves, but the possibility that they might end. As rumors of a Russia-Ukraine ceasefire intensify, Oleg Ogenko, a senior executive at A7A5, publicly stated that even if sanctions are lifted, the coin still has a reason to survive—faster and more convenient cross-border settlements. This sounds confident, but from another perspective, it also reveals unease: once the sanctions that underpin its existence disappear, what will it use to compete with the liquidity-dominant dollar-denominated stablecoins? The new Duma law, however, is not truly preparing for another stablecoin, but rather a national-level system for cross-border settlement of digital assets. In the past, Russia has relied more on market-based solutions like A7A5 in practice; in the future, whether it's a new compliant stablecoin, a regulated digital financial asset, or a digital ruble, they could all potentially play different roles within this framework, rather than relying on a single token already embroiled in sanctions for cross-border settlement capabilities. Even more coincidentally, the Central Bank of Russia also plans to further promote the wider application of the digital ruble. The almost simultaneous advancement of the institutional framework and the central bank digital currency signifies that Russia is attempting to gradually shift from a "shadow settlement network" that previously relied on spontaneous market formation to a state-led, regulated digital settlement system. A shadow token that survived through repeated maneuvering, a national law that has just gained legal status but requires strict documentation, and a digital ruble issued directly by the central bank—three lines are running simultaneously. What sanctions truly force out is never a single ingenious design, but rather a whole set of mutually reinforcing survival systems. Who will be eliminated first? And who will survive until the armistice?

data.title: Coinbase Opens New Singapore Office and Plans Major Hiring Drive Across Engineering and Institutional Sales
data.description:
Singapore Becomes Coinbase's Next Big Bet In Asia

Coinbase is putting fresh weight behind its Singapore business, with plans to lift its local headcount from around 150 to close to 200 staff by the end of 2026.

The Nasdaq-listed crypto exchange marked the milestone by opening a new office at One Raffles Quay on Wednesday, a move that country director Hassan Ahmed says reflects how central the city-state has become to the company's Asia-Pacific ambitions.

A new chapter begins.

Yesterday, we officially opened the doors to our permanent Singapore office.

Honoured to celebrate with Mr Kenneth Gay, Chief FinTech Officer of MAS, and Ms Seah Yueh Chinn, VP of Digital Industry Singapore at @singapore_edb — alongside friends from… pic.twitter.com/JKsDdRBQVI

— Coinbase Singapore 🛡️ (@CoinbaseSG) July 23, 2026

Which Teams Are Set To Grow

Speaking to The Business Times, Ahmed said the bulk of new roles will sit in engineering, customer service, relationship management and institutional sales.

The hiring push is notable given it runs opposite to Coinbase's global trajectory.

On 5 May 2026, the company cut roughly 14 per cent of its worldwide workforce, pointing to cost discipline in unpredictable markets and the growing role of artificial intelligence in its operations.

Management layers were also trimmed as part of that restructuring, with remaining staff redirected towards AI-focused work.

Why Is Coinbase Doubling Down On Singapore

Ahmed called Singapore "one of the world's most trusted financial hubs and one of Coinbase's fastest-growing international markets."

On the new office, he said:

"This new office reflects our long-term confidence in Singapore as a strategic hub for innovation, talent, and responsible growth across the Asia-Pacific, giving us the resources to work more closely with local authorities, invest in talent and scale partnerships."

He credited the country's early and clear rules for digital assets as a key draw, noting:

"It was much ahead of other jurisdictions and hubs that were also vying to be digital asset hubs at that time."

A supportive business climate, favourable tax policies and steady capital inflows were cited as additional reasons the exchange continues to invest there.

What's Driving Demand For Crypto In The Region

Ahmed pointed to two client groups fuelling growth, accredited investors and institutional players, both of which he said have sharpened their attention on digital assets and blockchain technology more broadly.

He described a "huge swell of interest" building around tokenisation as regulators worldwide take a more active role in shaping the space, citing the Genius Act in the United States, the European Union's Markets in Crypto-Assets framework and Hong Kong's stablecoin ordinance as examples.

Locally, the Monetary Authority of Singapore has introduced a tokenised bills pilot alongside its stablecoin regulatory framework.

Singapore plans to pilot tokenized MAS Bills settled with a wholesale CBDC in 2026. pic.twitter.com/jINEbvzfqK

— Coinsauce (@CoinsauceCrypto) November 13, 2025

On shifting user expectations, Ahmed said:

"Consumers and traders now have an expectation of 24/7 trading markets, and they want to apply this technology to tokenise assets to make them 24/7. They also want to use stablecoins to do instant settlement."

How Is Coinbase Positioned In Singapore Already

Coinbase secured its full major payment institution licence from the Monetary Authority of Singapore in October 2023, a step that opened the door to serving both individuals and institutions in the country.

Since then, it has backed the Singapore dollar-pegged stablecoin XSGD alongside StraitsX, and rolled out Coinbase Business to give companies access to stablecoin payment infrastructure.

Singapore's digital payment token licensing regime currently lists 38 approved entities, according to the MAS directory, after the regulator tightened requirements in 2025 and pushed unlicensed operators out of the market.

What Comes Next For AI And Digital Assets

Looking further ahead, Coinbase is examining how artificial intelligence and crypto could intersect, including giving AI agents their own stablecoin wallets so their blockchain activity can be tracked and audited.

AI is also being woven into internal engineering processes, though Ahmed stressed that human oversight will remain essential.

He said,

"Digital assets and AI are effectively colliding. We are very excited about the potential of AI."

How Coinbase's Stock Has Performed

The Singapore expansion arrives against a difficult backdrop for Coinbase shares, which have fallen 59 per cent over the past 12 months to trade at US$162.13, roughly S$208 based on current exchange rates.

data.title: Is Alex Atallah's "high-profile exit" scenario repeating itself?
data.description:
Author: Nancy, PANews

More than four years ago, Alex Atallah exited the NFT market at its peak; now, he is enjoying a high-profile moment in the AI ​​boom and is preparing to sell his AI model aggregation platform, OpenRouter, for a high price.

On July 23, according to the Wall Street Journal, payment giant Stripe is in talks to acquire OpenRouter, with a potential valuation close to $10 billion. If the deal goes through, it will be Alex Atallah's second successful attempt to build a multi-billion dollar company after his NFT platform, OpenSea. ...>

Stripe is reportedly planning to acquire OpenRouter, with a potential valuation of up to $10 billion. Rumors of OpenRouter being sold have been circulating for some time. Last week, several foreign media outlets, including The Information and Jawl, reported that OpenRouter had received acquisition offers from several large technology companies and was in discussions regarding a sale, with a potential transaction size of several billion dollars. According to the latest news from the Wall Street Journal, Stripe is considering acquiring the world's largest AI model aggregation platform. Sources familiar with the matter revealed that negotiations between the two parties may be announced soon, but the deal is still subject to change, and the possibility of negotiations breaking down or other potential buyers joining the bidding cannot be ruled out. Interestingly, Alex Atallah once described OpenRouter as "the Stripe of the AI ​​world." In his view, just as Stripe helps customers process various payments through a unified portal, OpenRouter aims to become a unified portal for enterprises to access different AI models, reducing the cost of switching between multiple AI vendors and avoiding being "locked in" by a single model vendor. In fact, OpenRouter and Stripe have already established a partnership. This potential transaction is also seen as an important step for the payment infrastructure giant to further extend into the AI ​​infrastructure field. It's worth noting that this could be another major acquisition for Stripe recently, following reports of its planned acquisition of PayPal. Currently, neither party has disclosed the specific transaction amount. However, according to sources, if the deal is finalized, OpenRouter's valuation at the time of sale could approach $10 billion. This figure will far exceed OpenRouter's valuation during its previous funding rounds. Founded just over three years ago, this AI infrastructure company has achieved rapid growth driven by the large-scale model wave. Public data shows that OpenRouter has completed three rounds of financing, raising over $150 million in total. In June 2025, OpenRouter announced the completion of a $40 million seed and Series A funding round, valuing the company at approximately $547 million post-money. On March 26, 2026, OpenRouter announced the completion of a $113 million Series B funding round, valuing the company at approximately $1.3 billion post-money. If this transaction ultimately goes through, it means that OpenRouter's valuation could increase nearly tenfold within months, joining the ranks of billion-dollar unicorns. The soaring value of OpenRouter stems not only from the explosive demand driven by the rapid expansion of its large-scale models but also from the increasing attention the AI ​​infrastructure sector is receiving from the capital market. Will this be a repeat of OpenSea's exit scenario? Why OpenRouter Chooses to "Sell Itself" This isn't the first time Alex Atallah has built a company worth tens of billions of dollars. Previously, this serial entrepreneur co-founded OpenSea, bringing NFTs from a niche community into the mainstream. At the height of the NFT craze, OpenSea grew from a little-known platform into the world's largest NFT marketplace, with a valuation exceeding $13 billion, and the two founders' net worth reaching approximately $2.2 billion. However, before the NFT market cooled significantly in 2022, Alex Atallah chose to leave OpenSea. Subsequently, as the industry bubble burst, OpenSea's valuation shrank dramatically, and the former NFT leader gradually lost its peak glory. Alex Atallah's early departure was seen by the market as a significant peak signal. Afterwards, Alex Atallah turned his attention to AI infrastructure, and his company, OpenRouter, became the largest hub in the AI ​​era. Currently, OpenRouter has integrated over 400 AI models, boasts approximately 10 million users, and processes over 200 trillion tokens monthly. This year, the number of tokens processed through its API has increased approximately tenfold. However, it's worth noting that while its business is growing rapidly, OpenRouter has not opted for an IPO, but rather pursued a potential sale. The reason is that this is a massive but limited-profit channel business. Currently, OpenRouter primarily profits by charging developers a platform service fee when they call its AI models, with a commission rate of approximately 5%-5.5%. Although its annualized AI inference consumption has reached hundreds of millions of dollars, as of April 2026, the company's annualized revenue is estimated at approximately $50 million. In other words, OpenRouter connects a huge AI demand market, but does not fully control the upstream of the value chain. As model capabilities become increasingly standardized, platforms are vulnerable to the rise of open-source models, ecosystem integration with cloud vendors, and price reductions directly from model suppliers, potentially putting continued pressure on profit margins. OpenRouter will find it difficult to tell a story of exponential growth and exceptionally high profits to the capital market. Meanwhile, competition in the AI ​​model aggregation sector is intensifying. For example, Meta's internal AI incubator overseas is developing a scheduling service to rival OpenRouter, aiming to reduce computational costs associated with code development; domestically, large model aggregation platforms such as Cheetah Mobile's EasyRouter and NetEase Youdao's ThinkFlow have emerged. Therefore, the current high valuation given to OpenRouter by the market is more about pricing its future potential than its current profitability. However, for potential buyers, OpenRouter's truly attractive asset may not be its current revenue scale, but rather its long-term accumulation of real-world AI usage data. By connecting hundreds of models and tens of millions of users, OpenRouter has accumulated a vast amount of usage data from real-world production environments, including performance differences between different models in actual tasks, developer preferences, price sensitivity, and the substitutability between open-source and closed-source models. Compared to lab test data, this real-world AI usage data is closer to market demand and more difficult to replicate quickly through short-term investment. This may be a key reason why large tech companies are willing to pay a premium for OpenRouter. From NFTs to AI, Alex Atallah has twice caught the wave of technological advancements. If OpenRouter is ultimately sold for tens of billions of dollars, does it signify a repricing of AI infrastructure value, or is it a signal of another cycle peak? The answer may still require time to prove.
